meta.title: Data Modeling — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Ten chapters that take you from 'what's a primary key' to deliberate schema design under load — keys, normalization, denormalization, constraints, multi-tenancy, JSONB, and zero-downtime evolution.

meta.title: What data modeling is
meta.subtitle: Schema design is the most expensive thing you ship. The columns you pick on day one outlast every framework, every refactor, every rewrite. Modeling well is mostly thinking before typing CREATE TABLE.

meta.title: Entities, attributes, relationships
meta.subtitle: Before SQL, before ORMs, draw the boxes and lines. ER thinking is twenty minutes that saves a year of refactoring.

meta.title: Keys
meta.subtitle: The primary key is the most expensive choice in your schema. Pick wrong and you spend years working around it. Pick right and you forget it exists.

meta.title: Normalization
meta.subtitle: 1NF, 2NF, 3NF — three rules that prevent the same data from being recorded twice. Ignore them and your schema rots from the inside as data drifts out of sync.

meta.title: Denormalization
meta.subtitle: Sometimes the same fact lives in two places on purpose. Done well, it makes hot queries fly. Done badly, it creates the exact drift normalization was designed to prevent.

meta.title: Constraints
meta.subtitle: The schema is your last line of defense against application bugs. Constraints encode the rules that should always be true — Postgres enforces them whether your app remembers to or not.

meta.title: Time and soft delete
meta.subtitle: Timestamps that survive time zones, soft delete that doesn't poison every query, and history tables that answer 'what did this look like last Tuesday?'

meta.title: Multi-tenancy
meta.subtitle: Three architectures for serving many customers from one app: single database with a tenant column, schema per tenant, or a database per tenant. Each has a different cost curve.

meta.title: JSONB and the schemaless trap
meta.subtitle: JSONB is one of Postgres's best features. Used well, it's how you stop fighting your schema. Used badly, it's how you end up with a schemaless mess inside a relational database.

meta.title: Schema evolution
meta.subtitle: Every schema change against live data is a deploy. The expand/contract pattern is how you change tables that have a million rows and a thousand concurrent writers without downtime.

meta.title: Storage Engines
meta.subtitle: How databases actually store data on disk — pages, heaps, and the tradeoffs between read and write optimization.

meta.title: B-Trees & Indexes
meta.subtitle: The data structure behind every relational database index — how B-trees keep data sorted and queries fast.

meta.title: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)
meta.subtitle: How databases survive crashes without losing data — the log-first strategy that makes ACID possible.

meta.title: MVCC — Multi-Version Concurrency Control
meta.subtitle: How databases let readers and writers work simultaneously without blocking — snapshots, visibility rules, and isolation levels.

meta.title: Query Planning & Execution
meta.subtitle: How the database turns your SQL into an execution plan — parsing, optimization, and why EXPLAIN is your best friend.

meta.title: LSM Trees
meta.subtitle: The write-optimized alternative to B-trees — how RocksDB, Cassandra, and LevelDB handle massive write throughput.

meta.title: Transactions & ACID
meta.subtitle: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the guarantees that make databases reliable and how they're implemented.

meta.title: Replication & Sharding
meta.subtitle: Scaling databases beyond a single machine — leader-follower replication, consensus, and horizontal partitioning.

meta.title: Containers & Docker
meta.subtitle: What containers actually are — namespaces, cgroups, layers — and how Docker packages your app to run anywhere.

meta.title: Kubernetes Essentials
meta.subtitle: Pods, Deployments, Services — the core building blocks of container orchestration at scale.

meta.title: CI/CD Pipelines
meta.subtitle: Automate building, testing, and deploying your code — GitHub Actions, pipeline design, and deployment strategies.

meta.title: Infrastructure as Code
meta.subtitle: Terraform, declarative infrastructure, state management — define your cloud resources in version-controlled files.

meta.title: Monitoring & Observability
meta.subtitle: Metrics, logs, traces — the three pillars of understanding what your system is doing in production.

meta.title: Cloud Networking
meta.subtitle: VPCs, subnets, security groups, load balancers — how to design a secure cloud network architecture.

meta.title: Secrets & Configuration
meta.subtitle: Environment variables, secret managers, config as code — how to handle sensitive data without leaking it.

meta.title: Reliability & SRE Practices
meta.subtitle: SLOs, error budgets, incident response, and chaos engineering — keeping systems running when things go wrong.

meta.title: Disaster Recovery — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: RTO and RPO, pg_dump and WAL-G backups, restore drills, multi-region replication, and runbooks that work at 3am.

meta.title: RTO, RPO, and What They Actually Mean
meta.subtitle: Two numbers that define your recovery requirements — and why getting them wrong makes your DR plan useless.

meta.title: Backup Strategies
meta.subtitle: pg_dump for logical backups, WAL-G for continuous archival, S3 lifecycle for retention — the mechanics of actually keeping your data.

meta.title: Restore Drills
meta.subtitle: How to actually test your backups — the runbook structure, what to verify, and how to make drills a regular practice.

meta.title: Multi-Region Replication
meta.subtitle: Streaming replication, logical replication, and the trade-offs of active-passive vs active-active across regions.

meta.title: Runbooks & Incident Response
meta.subtitle: Writing runbooks people will actually use, incident commander patterns, post-mortems that produce real change.

meta.title: Arrays & Strings
meta.subtitle: Master the two most fundamental data structures — contiguous memory, indexing, and common manipulation patterns.

meta.title: Hash Maps & Sets
meta.subtitle: Turn O(n) lookups into O(1) with hash-based data structures — the most practically useful tool in your kit.

meta.title: Linked Lists
meta.subtitle: Understand pointer-based data structures — nodes, traversal, and why they matter for queues, LRU caches, and more.

meta.title: Stacks & Queues
meta.subtitle: LIFO and FIFO — two simple abstractions that power undo systems, BFS, task schedulers, and expression parsing.

meta.title: Binary Trees & BSTs
meta.subtitle: Recursive thinking, tree traversals, and binary search trees — the gateway to hierarchical data.

meta.title: Graphs

meta.subtitle: Merge sort, quicksort, and when to use which — plus the patterns that emerge from sorted data.

meta.title: Dynamic Programming
meta.subtitle: Break complex problems into overlapping subproblems — the technique behind shortest paths, text diffing, and optimization.

meta.subtitle: Zero to professional penetration tester. Recon, exploitation, post-exploitation, reporting — with real labs.

meta.subtitle: Terminal mastery, file permissions, bash scripting, and the tools that ship on Kali Linux.

meta.subtitle: Docker escape techniques, Kubernetes attacks, privileged container abuse, secrets in images, and hardening.

meta.subtitle: AFL++, LibFuzzer, coverage-guided fuzzing, finding 0-days, code auditing for security, and structured vulnerability research.

meta.subtitle: SAST tools, manual code auditing, threat modeling, finding vulnerabilities in real codebases, and building security into the SDLC.

meta.title: Functions & Error Handling
meta.subtitle: Go's approach to functions and errors is radically different from most languages — explicit, composable, and impossible to ignore.

meta.title: Pointers & Memory
meta.subtitle: Pointers let you share data without copying it — understand them once and never be confused by & and * again.

meta.title: Structs & Methods
meta.subtitle: Go doesn't have classes — it has structs with methods. Simpler, more explicit, and surprisingly powerful.

meta.title: Interfaces & Polymorphism
meta.subtitle: Go interfaces are implicit — no 'implements' keyword needed. This changes everything about how you design software.

meta.title: Generics
meta.subtitle: Write code that works with any type — Go 1.18's biggest feature eliminates boilerplate without sacrificing type safety.

meta.title: Strings, Runes & Encoding
meta.subtitle: Go strings are UTF-8 byte slices — knowing this prevents an entire class of bugs when handling international text, emojis, and binary data.

meta.title: Concurrency with Goroutines
meta.subtitle: Go's concurrency model is its killer feature — goroutines and channels make concurrent programming feel natural, not terrifying.

meta.subtitle: Context, sync primitives, worker pools, and the patterns that power Go at scale — from Uber to Cloudflare.

meta.subtitle: JSON, HTTP clients, databases, and file I/O — the bread and butter of every Go backend service.

meta.subtitle: JWT authentication, middleware chains, CORS, rate limiting — the security and cross-cutting concerns every API needs.

meta.title: Database Patterns
meta.subtitle: Migrations, repository pattern, connection pooling, and query builders — the database layer that scales.

meta.title: gRPC & Protocol Buffers
meta.subtitle: High-performance service-to-service communication — gRPC is what REST wants to be when services talk to each other.

meta.title: WebSockets & Real-Time
meta.subtitle: Build real-time features in Go — chat, live updates, notifications — using WebSockets and Go's concurrency model.

meta.title: Error Handling at Scale
meta.subtitle: Beyond 'if err != nil' — error wrapping strategies, domain errors, error budgets, and patterns from large Go codebases.

meta.title: Testing Strategies
meta.subtitle: Unit tests, integration tests, test fixtures, mocking, and testcontainers — testing that catches real bugs, not just checkbox coverage.

meta.title: Observability
meta.subtitle: Logs, metrics, and traces — the three pillars that let you understand what your Go service is doing in production.

meta.title: Production Go
meta.subtitle: Logging, configuration, graceful shutdown, profiling, and deployment — everything between 'it works on my machine' and running at scale.

meta.title: Building CLI Tools
meta.subtitle: Go's single-binary output makes it perfect for CLI tools — build developer tools, automation scripts, and DevOps utilities.

meta.title: Design Patterns in Go
meta.subtitle: Go's simplicity changes how you apply classic patterns — no classes, no inheritance, just composition and interfaces.

meta.title: Security Best Practices
meta.subtitle: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, secrets management, and cryptography — secure your Go services against real-world attacks.

meta.title: Case Study: URL Shortener
meta.subtitle: Build a complete URL shortener from scratch — applying everything you've learned: HTTP, database, caching, testing, and deployment.

meta.title: GraphQL Building — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Ten chapters that go from 'a query is just a string' to a self-hosted GraphQL server with batched resolvers, auth, subscriptions, and depth limits behind nginx.

meta.title: What GraphQL is and when to use it
meta.subtitle: GraphQL is not a database, not a transport, and not a replacement for HTTP. It is a query language and a typed contract — and that distinction shapes every decision you make from here on.

meta.title: Schema-first design
meta.subtitle: The schema is your contract with every client that ever exists. The decisions you make in the first afternoon — types, nullability, IDs — are the ones you live with for years.

meta.title: Running your first server
meta.subtitle: graphql-yoga, end-to-end, in sixty lines. By the end of this chapter you will have a real GraphQL server on your laptop, queried with curl, talking to Postgres.

meta.title: Resolvers and the execution tree
meta.subtitle: A resolver is a function that returns a value. Stack them in a tree, and that tree is your API. Once you see the walk, every weird GraphQL bug becomes obvious.

meta.title: The N+1 problem
meta.subtitle: Your GraphQL server runs eleven SQL queries when it should run two. Every backend team learns this the hard way. This chapter is the diagnosis — chapter 6 is the cure.

meta.title: DataLoader
meta.subtitle: DataLoader is a tiny library that fixes the N+1 problem by batching loads inside an event loop tick, and caching by key for the duration of one request. Once you wire it in, your resolvers stay clean and the SQL graph collapses.

meta.title: Mutations, input types, validation
meta.subtitle: Writes are not just queries with side effects. They need input types, validation, transactions, idempotency, and a return shape that lets clients update their cache without a second fetch.

meta.title: Authentication and authorization
meta.subtitle: Auth in GraphQL is the same as in REST — JWTs or sessions on HTTP, identity on context — except every field is its own little endpoint that needs an authorization check. Get the layering right or it will haunt you.

meta.title: Subscriptions over WebSockets
meta.subtitle: Subscriptions are realtime queries — clients open a long-lived connection and the server pushes events as they happen. Different transport, different lifecycle, different failure modes than queries and mutations.

meta.title: Production hardening and self-host
meta.subtitle: Depth limits, complexity limits, persisted queries, error sanitisation, federation, and the full self-hosted nginx deploy. Everything between 'works on my laptop' and 'survives a hostile internet.'

meta.title: gRPC Building — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Ten chapters that go from 'protobuf is a config language' to a polyglot, mTLS-secured, observable gRPC service running behind nginx, with streaming RPCs and tight deadlines.

meta.title: What gRPC is and when to use it
meta.subtitle: gRPC is HTTP/2, plus protobuf, plus codegen. That trio gives you a typed, fast, polyglot RPC system. Knowing what each layer is doing is half the skill of using it well.

meta.title: Protocol Buffers
meta.subtitle: Protobuf is the schema language and the wire format. Get the schema rules right and your services stay compatible for a decade. Get them wrong and one bad commit breaks every client at once.

meta.title: HTTP/2 underneath
meta.subtitle: gRPC is HTTP/2 with a particular set of headers and a particular way of framing protobuf bytes. Knowing what HTTP/2 actually does explains every gRPC failure mode you will hit in production.

meta.title: Your first server and client
meta.subtitle: End-to-end Go gRPC in one chapter — proto, codegen, server, client, reflection, grpcurl. By the end you have a real binary you could deploy.

meta.title: Polyglot — Node and Python clients
meta.subtitle: The same `.proto`, three languages. The whole pitch of gRPC is that the wire is shared and the codegen is free. Once you have done it once it stops feeling like magic.

meta.title: Streaming RPCs
meta.subtitle: Streams are the part of gRPC where it stops resembling REST. Server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional — three shapes that change what kind of services you can build.

meta.title: Errors, deadlines, metadata
meta.subtitle: Status codes are a fixed set, deadlines flow with context, metadata rides every call. The three together turn a working gRPC service into one that's debuggable and survivable.

meta.title: Interceptors
meta.subtitle: Interceptors are gRPC's middleware. Cross-cutting concerns — auth, logging, tracing, retries, panic recovery — live here, once, for every RPC. Get the layering right and your handlers stay tiny.

meta.title: TLS and mTLS
meta.subtitle: Server TLS encrypts the wire and proves the server's identity. Mutual TLS adds the same proof for clients. Both ride on the same handshake — and once you have a small CA, both are a few lines of Go.

meta.title: Production self-host
meta.subtitle: Load balancing that respects HTTP/2, observability you actually use, health checks the framework supports, and the full systemd + nginx deploy on a VPS.

meta.title: Horizontal Scaling — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Stateless services, load balancers, auto-scaling, database bottlenecks, and WebSocket fan-out across instances.

meta.title: Stateless Services
meta.subtitle: Why stateless is the prerequisite for horizontal scaling — and how to extract state from your application so any instance can handle any request.

meta.title: Load Balancers
meta.subtitle: Layer 4 vs Layer 7, algorithms, health checks, connection draining — the mechanics of distributing traffic across instances.

meta.title: Auto-Scaling
meta.subtitle: Scaling out on demand — target tracking, scheduled scaling, scale-in protection, and the metrics that actually drive good decisions.

meta.title: Scaling the Database Layer
meta.subtitle: Read replicas, connection pooling, and why the database is almost always the horizontal scaling bottleneck.

meta.title: WebSockets & Shared State at Scale
meta.subtitle: How to handle persistent connections, pub/sub fan-out, and the Redis adapter that makes Socket.io work across instances.

meta.title: Infrastructure as Code — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Ansible playbooks first, then Terraform against your own infrastructure.

meta.title: Why Infrastructure as Code
meta.subtitle: The problems with manual infrastructure — drift, undocumented state, unrepeatable environments — and how IaC solves them.

meta.title: Ansible Fundamentals
meta.subtitle: Inventories, playbooks, roles, and variables — configuring servers reliably without installing an agent.

meta.title: Terraform Fundamentals
meta.subtitle: Resources, state, providers, modules, and the plan/apply workflow that makes cloud provisioning reproducible.

meta.title: Terraform Patterns & Production
meta.subtitle: Module composition, environments, secrets, drift detection, and the practices that keep large Terraform codebases manageable.

meta.title: Linux & VPS Basics — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Twelve chapters that take you from a brand-new VPS to a hardened, monitored, ready-to-run-real-software box. No managed cloud.

meta.title: Picking a VPS
meta.subtitle: What a VPS actually is, how to size one, and how to provision your first box without locking yourself into anyone's ecosystem.

meta.title: First Login & SSH Hardening
meta.subtitle: Generate a key, disable password login, lock down sshd, and put fail2ban in front. The single most important hour you will ever spend on a VPS.

meta.title: The Linux Filesystem
meta.subtitle: Where everything lives, why it lives there, and the permissions model that decides what your processes can touch.

meta.title: Processes & Signals
meta.subtitle: What a Linux process actually is, how fork and exec build the entire universe, and the signals that decide whether your app shuts down cleanly or dies screaming.

meta.title: systemd
meta.subtitle: Write a unit file. Restart on crash. Survive reboot. Read the journal. The supervisor that runs everything on a modern Linux box.

meta.title: Sockets, Ports, and What's Listening
meta.subtitle: Every network service is a socket. Every open port is a socket someone bound. The four tools that tell you exactly what your VPS is exposing.

meta.title: Firewall Fundamentals
meta.subtitle: How packets actually flow through the kernel, and how to write nftables rules that allow exactly what you want and reject everything else.

meta.title: Users, Groups, and Sudo
meta.subtitle: How Linux identifies who is doing what, why services run as their own users, and how sudo gives you root without making you root.

meta.title: Logs & journalctl
meta.subtitle: Where every line of output goes on a modern Linux box, how to query it, how to keep the disk from filling up, and when to ship logs off the host.

meta.title: Resource Limits
meta.subtitle: ulimit, cgroups, and the OOM killer — three layers of resource control that decide whether your services share the box politely or fight to the death.

meta.title: Cron & systemd Timers
meta.subtitle: Two ways to run code on a schedule. Which to pick, how to write each, and how to keep scheduled jobs from silently failing for months.

meta.title: Production Checklist
meta.subtitle: Every step from this track, in one runbook. Provision a fresh VPS and end the day with a hardened, monitored, ready-to-deploy box.

meta.title: Load Balancing — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: HAProxy and nginx. L4 vs L7, health checks, sticky sessions, weighted routing.

meta.title: L4 vs L7 Load Balancing
meta.subtitle: The difference between routing packets and routing requests — when to use each layer and what each can see.

meta.title: Load Balancing Algorithms
meta.subtitle: Round-robin, least connections, IP hash, weighted routing — when each algorithm fits and what happens when backends aren't equal.

meta.title: Health Checks
meta.subtitle: Active vs passive detection, failure thresholds, graceful draining — keeping dead backends out of rotation.

meta.title: SSL Termination
meta.subtitle: TLS at the load balancer, certificate management, HTTPS between LB and backends, and the trade-offs of offloading vs end-to-end encryption.

meta.title: HAProxy in Depth
meta.subtitle: Frontend/backend config, ACLs, the stats page, runtime API, and the patterns that make HAProxy the go-to proxy for demanding deployments.

meta.title: Advanced Load Balancing Patterns
meta.subtitle: Blue-green deployments, circuit breakers, global load balancing, and anycast — patterns for deployments that can't go wrong.

meta.title: Messaging & Queues — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Self-host RabbitMQ or NATS. Then run a Kafka cluster across 3 VPS.

meta.title: Why Messaging Systems
meta.subtitle: The problems direct HTTP calls create at scale — and how async messaging solves coupling, backpressure, and reliability.

meta.title: RabbitMQ
meta.subtitle: Exchanges, queues, bindings, dead letter exchanges — the AMQP model and how to use it for reliable task processing.

meta.title: Kafka
meta.subtitle: Topics, partitions, consumer groups, retention — the distributed log that makes replay and high-throughput event streaming possible.

meta.title: NATS
meta.subtitle: Core pub/sub, JetStream persistence, KV store, and running a 3-node cluster — the fast path to reliable messaging without Kafka complexity.

meta.title: Messaging Patterns
meta.subtitle: Saga, inbox/outbox, event-driven choreography vs orchestration — the patterns that make distributed systems reliable despite partial failure.

meta.title: Microservices — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: When to split a monolith, gRPC between services, service discovery with Consul.

meta.title: Monolith vs Microservices
meta.subtitle: The real costs of splitting — when a monolith is the right call, when it isn't, and how to identify the seams before you cut.

meta.title: gRPC Between Services
meta.subtitle: Protocol Buffers, generated clients, streaming, and why gRPC beats REST for internal service communication.

meta.title: Service Discovery
meta.subtitle: How services find each other — DNS-based discovery, Consul, client-side vs server-side load balancing, and health-integrated routing.

meta.title: API Gateway
meta.subtitle: The entry point for all external traffic — routing, auth, rate limiting, request transformation, and what not to put in a gateway.

meta.title: Inter-Service Reliability
meta.subtitle: Timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, and bulkheads — the patterns that prevent one slow service from cascading into a full outage.

meta.title: How the Internet Works
meta.subtitle: Packets, routers, and the physical infrastructure that connects billions of devices — the big picture before diving into protocols.

meta.title: DNS — The Internet's Phone Book
meta.subtitle: How domain names become IP addresses — recursive resolvers, authoritative servers, caching, and DNS record types.

meta.title: TCP/IP — Reliable Delivery
meta.subtitle: The three-way handshake, flow control, congestion avoidance — how TCP guarantees every byte arrives in order.

meta.title: UDP — Speed Over Reliability
meta.subtitle: When losing a few packets is better than waiting — video calls, gaming, DNS lookups, and building your own reliability.

meta.title: TLS & HTTPS
meta.subtitle: Certificates, handshakes, and encryption — how TLS keeps your data private and verifies you're talking to the right server.

meta.title: HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3
meta.subtitle: The evolution of the web's protocol — from text-based request/response to multiplexed streams over QUIC.

meta.title: WebSockets & Real-Time
meta.subtitle: Full-duplex communication over a single connection — chat, live updates, collaborative editing, and when not to use them.

meta.title: Network Security
meta.subtitle: CORS, CSP, MITM attacks, firewalls — practical security at the network layer that every developer should understand.

meta.title: Observability — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Logs with journald, metrics with Prometheus and Grafana, traces with Jaeger and OpenTelemetry.

meta.title: The Three Pillars of Observability
meta.subtitle: Logs, metrics, and traces — what each tells you, where each falls short, and how they work together to answer 'what's broken and why.'

meta.title: Structured Logging
meta.subtitle: JSON logs, log levels, correlation IDs, log aggregation with Loki — building logs you can actually search in production.

meta.title: Prometheus & Grafana
meta.subtitle: Instrumenting applications, writing PromQL, building dashboards, and alerting on what actually matters.

meta.title: Distributed Tracing
meta.subtitle: OpenTelemetry instrumentation, trace propagation across services, Jaeger, and using traces to find what logs and metrics can't.

meta.title: Alerting & On-Call
meta.subtitle: Alerts that fire when users are impacted, not when metrics twitch — SLO-based alerting, runbooks, and on-call practices that don't burn people out.

meta.title: Orchestration — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Run a k3s cluster across your own VPS fleet. Not EKS, not GKE — your hardware.

meta.title: Kubernetes Fundamentals
meta.subtitle: Pods, deployments, services, and the control loop — what Kubernetes actually does and the primitives that everything else is built on.

meta.title: k3s on Your Own VPS
meta.subtitle: A production-grade Kubernetes cluster on bare metal — k3s setup, node joining, persistent storage, and why k3s beats managed Kubernetes for cost-conscious teams.

meta.title: Kubernetes Workloads
meta.subtitle: StatefulSets, DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs — the right object for each class of workload and when Deployments aren't enough.

meta.title: Kubernetes Networking & Security
meta.subtitle: NetworkPolicies, RBAC, Pod Security Standards, Secrets management, and the default-deny posture that keeps clusters hardened.

meta.title: GitOps & Deployments
meta.subtitle: ArgoCD, Helm, progressive delivery with Argo Rollouts — cluster state as code, automated sync, and deployments that can't silently go wrong.

meta.title: Performance Engineering — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Profiling with pprof and perf, flamegraphs, latency budgets, p99 thinking.

meta.title: Latency Thinking
meta.subtitle: P50 vs P99 vs P999, the tail latency problem, latency budgets, and why averages hide the failures that matter most.

meta.title: Profiling
meta.subtitle: Node.js --prof, Go pprof, Linux perf, and flamegraphs — finding where the time actually goes before guessing at optimizations.

meta.title: Database Performance
meta.subtitle: Query plans, index strategy, N+1 queries, connection pool tuning — the database is almost always the bottleneck.

meta.title: Caching
meta.subtitle: Cache levels, eviction policies, cache stampede, cache invalidation — and why caching is the most over-applied and under-thought optimization.

meta.title: Load Testing
meta.subtitle: k6, autocannon, realistic traffic models, finding the breaking point — and why load testing in staging is different from production.

meta.title: Replication & Sharding — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Postgres streaming replicas by hand. Manual shard routing without Vitess.

meta.title: Replication Fundamentals
meta.subtitle: Why replication exists, synchronous vs asynchronous, WAL-based streaming, and the consistency trade-offs every replica introduces.

meta.title: Postgres Streaming Replication by Hand
meta.subtitle: Setting up a primary, configuring pg_basebackup, streaming WAL to a standby — no managed service, no Patroni, just Postgres.

meta.title: Sharding Concepts
meta.subtitle: When replication isn't enough, partition keys, shard routing strategies, and the operational complexity you accept when you shard.

meta.title: Manual Shard Routing
meta.subtitle: Building a shard router in application code — connection management, transaction boundaries, migration strategies, and avoiding common pitfalls.

meta.title: Postgres Table Partitioning
meta.subtitle: Range, list, and hash partitioning — the single-server answer to large tables that buys you most of what sharding promises without the operational cost.

meta.title: Search — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Build an inverted index from scratch. Then self-host Meilisearch or Elasticsearch.

meta.title: How Search Works
meta.subtitle: Inverted indexes, tokenization, TF-IDF, BM25 — the mechanics behind every search engine from Postgres full-text to Elasticsearch.

meta.title: Postgres Full-Text Search
meta.subtitle: tsvector, GIN indexes, ranking, highlighting, and when Postgres search is all you need.

meta.title: Meilisearch
meta.subtitle: Self-hosting, indexing documents, typo tolerance, faceted search, and keeping Meilisearch in sync with your database.

meta.title: Elasticsearch
meta.subtitle: Mappings, analyzers, the Query DSL, aggregations, and running a production cluster — for when Meilisearch isn't enough.

meta.title: Search in Practice
meta.subtitle: Relevance tuning, synonyms, personalization, analytics, and the operational patterns that keep search working well over time.

meta.title: 8-Week Roadmap: Fullstack → SRE
meta.subtitle: A solid two-month plan to convert a working fullstack engineer into a junior-SRE-ready operator. Daily breakdown, real labs, and a final capstone.

meta.title: What SRE Actually Is
meta.subtitle: Class SRE implements DevOps. The error-budget contract, toil cap, and the embedded engineer model that makes Google's reliability work.

meta.title: SLIs, SLOs & Error Budgets
meta.subtitle: Pick the right SLI, set an SLO that survives lawyer review, and burn the budget the way Google's CRE team does it.

meta.title: Golden Signals, RED, and USE
meta.subtitle: The three monitoring frameworks that actually matter, when to use each, and the Prometheus + Grafana stack that exposes them all.

meta.title: Incident Response & On-Call
meta.subtitle: ICS roles, severity classification, comms cadence, and the on-call rotation that doesn't burn engineers out.

meta.title: Blameless Postmortems
meta.subtitle: The full template Google, Etsy, and Stripe use, with action-item discipline that prevents the same incident twice.

meta.title: Capacity Planning & Load Testing
meta.subtitle: Little's Law, Universal Scalability Law, headroom, and a real k6 + Locust load test you can run today.

meta.title: Production Readiness Reviews
meta.subtitle: The PRR checklist that prevents 80% of preventable launches-into-fire, with a real launch gate Terraform module.

meta.title: Chaos Engineering
meta.subtitle: Hypothesis-driven failure injection from Netflix Simian Army to Chaos Mesh, with real experiments and a safety harness.

meta.title: Disaster Recovery & Backups
meta.subtitle: RTO, RPO, multi-region failover, and the restore drill that proves your backups exist.

meta.title: Toil & Automation
meta.subtitle: Measuring toil, the 50% cap, and the automation taxonomy from one-off scripts to self-healing operators.

meta.title: Scaling & Distributed Systems — 8-Week Companion Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Zero to designing, building, and operating scalable systems. 1–2 hrs/day, 8 weeks, 8 real projects, 5 case studies, mini-YouTube capstone.

meta.title: Linux Performance Mastery
meta.subtitle: From `top` to `perf`, `bpftrace`, and flame graphs. The senior-SRE toolkit for diagnosing latency, CPU, memory, and I/O at the kernel level — without restarting anything in production.

meta.title: Network Engineering for SREs
meta.subtitle: BGP, anycast, ECMP, CDN internals, packet capture, and TCP at scale. The networking layer where 'random' production weirdness actually lives.

meta.title: Database Internals for SREs
meta.subtitle: MVCC, replication lag, hot rows, query plans, B-tree vs LSM, connection pools at scale. The DB knowledge that separates 'I run Postgres' from 'I keep Postgres up under fire.'

meta.title: Distributed Systems Theory for SREs
meta.subtitle: CAP, PACELC, FLP, Raft, Paxos, gossip, vector clocks, CRDTs, fencing tokens. The theory that explains why your distributed system breaks the way it does.

meta.title: Kubernetes at Scale
meta.subtitle: 1,000+ node clusters, multi-tenancy, RBAC, NetworkPolicy, OPA/Kyverno, GitOps, etcd tuning. The operating model when 'just run kubectl apply' is no longer a strategy.

meta.title: Service Mesh Internals
meta.subtitle: Envoy, Istio, Linkerd, sidecar vs ambient, mTLS, xDS, retries, circuit breakers, traffic shifting. What a mesh actually does and when it earns its complexity.

meta.title: FinOps & Cost Engineering
meta.subtitle: Unit economics, rightsizing, spot, savings plans, cost-aware SLOs. The senior SRE skill that turns 'the cloud bill is too high' into a tracked, owned, falling number.

meta.title: Reliability Culture & SRE Org Design
meta.subtitle: Staff+ SRE work, embedding, charters, blame-aware orgs, mentoring, sustainable on-call. The non-technical lever that makes or breaks every reliability program.

meta.title: 12-Month Mastery Roadmap — Junior SRE → Senior/Staff
meta.subtitle: The year-long plan that picks up where the 8-week roadmap stops. Monthly milestones, real production projects, deep reading, and the artifacts that prove staff-level capability.

meta.title: File & Object Storage — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Local disk, NFS, then self-host MinIO. Build a CDN with nginx and Varnish.

meta.title: Storage Primitives
meta.subtitle: Block, file, and object storage — what each abstraction provides, where each breaks down, and how to choose.

meta.title: Self-Hosted Object Storage with MinIO
meta.subtitle: Running MinIO on your own hardware, S3-compatible API, distributed mode, lifecycle policies, and using it from application code.

meta.title: File Uploads
meta.subtitle: Multipart parsing, validation, virus scanning, direct-to-storage upload, and processing pipelines.

meta.title: CDN with nginx and Varnish
meta.subtitle: Cache static assets close to users — self-hosted edge caching with nginx proxy_cache and Varnish as a reverse proxy.

meta.title: Storage in Practice
meta.subtitle: Cost optimization, access control, signed URLs for private content, backup strategy, and operational patterns.

meta.title: Testing Strategy — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Unit, integration, contract, e2e. Load test with k6 and wrk. Property-based testing.

meta.title: Testing Strategy
meta.subtitle: The testing pyramid, what each layer tests, and how to avoid the traps that make test suites slow and fragile.

meta.title: Unit Testing
meta.subtitle: Testing pure logic in isolation — Vitest, assertion patterns, test doubles, and what makes a good unit test.

meta.title: Integration Testing
meta.subtitle: Testing real boundaries — database queries, HTTP handlers, message queues — without mocking what matters.

meta.title: Contract Testing
meta.subtitle: Verify that services agree on the API contract without deploying them together — Pact for consumer-driven contracts.

meta.title: End-to-End Testing
meta.subtitle: Testing user journeys in a real browser — Playwright setup, selectors, auth, CI, and keeping e2e tests fast and reliable.

meta.title: Property-Based Testing
meta.subtitle: Generate hundreds of random inputs automatically — fast-check finds edge cases your example tests miss.

meta.title: TLS & Certificates — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Eight chapters that take TLS from mystery to muscle memory — what it actually does, why every part exists, and how to issue, renew, and serve real certificates without a managed service.

meta.title: What TLS Actually Is
meta.subtitle: TLS does two jobs: it encrypts your traffic, and it proves the server is who it says it is. The two halves work together — neither alone is enough.

meta.title: The TLS Handshake
meta.subtitle: ClientHello, ServerHello, key exchange, finished. The five-message conversation that turns a TCP connection into a secure session — and how TLS 1.3 cut it in half.

meta.title: Certificates and the Chain of Trust
meta.subtitle: What lives inside a .pem file, what a CSR is, why intermediate certificates exist, and how a browser walks the chain to a root it already trusts.

meta.title: Let's Encrypt & ACME
meta.subtitle: The protocol that lets a free CA issue 350 million certificates a year. Account, order, challenge, finalize, download — what every certbot run does behind the scenes.

meta.title: Issuing Your First Cert with certbot
meta.subtitle: From bare DNS A record to working HTTPS in ten minutes. Concrete commands, every flag explained, every common failure addressed.

meta.title: Wildcard Certs & DNS-01
meta.subtitle: When HTTP validation isn't enough — issuing wildcard certs, automating DNS-01 with provider plugins, and the security tradeoffs of API tokens that can edit DNS.

meta.title: Configuring nginx for TLS
meta.subtitle: A complete TLS server block — protocols, ciphers, OCSP stapling, HSTS, modern key types, perfect forward secrecy. The config that scores A+ without copy-paste cargo.

meta.title: Renewal, Monitoring, and Rotation
meta.subtitle: Surviving past 90 days. Renewal hooks, expiration monitoring, key rotation, and the runbook for the night your cert quietly expired and now nobody can reach the site.

meta.title: Web Server Fundamentals — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Ten chapters that build a working understanding of web servers — from raw TCP sockets to a tuned, hardened nginx in front of a real backend.

meta.title: What is a Web Server
meta.subtitle: Anatomy of an HTTP request from socket to response. The four stages every web server runs through, and the names for the parts.

meta.title: HTTP from a Raw Socket
meta.subtitle: Speak HTTP by hand with netcat. Then write a 60-line Go server that does exactly the same thing — no framework, no surprises.

meta.title: Building a Real HTTP/1.1 Parser
meta.subtitle: Request bodies, Content-Length, chunked transfer encoding, and the dozen edge cases that turn a toy parser into one you would trust in production.

meta.title: Concurrency Models
meta.subtitle: Process per request, thread per request, prefork, event loop, hybrid. The five ways web servers handle thousands of concurrent connections — and why each one exists.

meta.title: Static Files & MIME
meta.subtitle: How a server turns a file path into bytes on the wire — content types, ETags, conditional requests, and the cache headers that keep every browser fast.

meta.title: nginx Fundamentals
meta.subtitle: Install, structure the config, write server blocks, understand locations and includes. The nginx mental model that holds for every advanced feature.

meta.title: Reverse Proxy
meta.subtitle: nginx in front of your application server. The headers that matter, the timeouts that save you, and the upstream pool that handles failure.

meta.title: Access & Error Logs
meta.subtitle: Customize nginx log formats, understand what each field means, find requests by status or duration, and tell access logs apart from error logs the right way.

meta.title: Edge Caching with nginx
meta.subtitle: proxy_cache, microcaching, stale-while-revalidate, and the cache key rules that turn nginx into a CDN you control.

meta.title: Performance & Hardening
meta.subtitle: Workers, sendfile, gzip and brotli, security headers, rate limiting, connection limits, body size caps. The dials that turn a working nginx into a fast, defensible one.

meta.title: Webhooks — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Ten chapters that go from 'webhooks are just HTTP POST requests' to a self-hosted, signed, retried, idempotent webhook system that survives the receiver being down for hours.

meta.title: What webhooks are and when to use them
meta.subtitle: A webhook is an HTTP POST you make to somebody else's server when something happens. The protocol is trivial; the failure modes are not.

meta.title: Event contract design
meta.subtitle: The shape of your event payload becomes a contract with everyone who integrates. The decisions you make in the first afternoon are the ones you live with for years.

meta.title: Sending webhooks
meta.subtitle: End-to-end producer in Go in sixty lines. By the end you have a binary that POSTs JSON to a URL, handles non-2xx, and times out cleanly. Signing, retries, and the outbox come later.

meta.title: Signing payloads
meta.subtitle: An unsigned webhook is a public POST endpoint. Anyone who guesses the URL can forge events. HMAC over a canonical string with a timestamp is the simple, correct fix.

meta.title: Verifying signatures
meta.subtitle: The receiver's job is to read the raw body, recompute the HMAC, compare in constant time, and reject events older than the replay window. Tiny code, easy to get wrong.

meta.title: Retries and backoff
meta.subtitle: A delivery that fails once is normal. A delivery that fails ten times in a hot loop takes down your service and your customer's. Exponential backoff with jitter and a hard deadline is the simple, correct fix.

meta.title: Idempotency on the receiver
meta.subtitle: At-least-once delivery means duplicates. The receiver's job is to process exactly once anyway. The inbox pattern — dedupe keys, atomic claim, idempotent side effects — is how.

meta.title: Delivery guarantees and the dead-letter queue
meta.subtitle: Some events never deliver. The dead-letter queue is where they go, the dashboard is where humans see them, and the manual replay is how you recover. None of these are optional.

meta.title: Observability and replay
meta.subtitle: Webhooks are not a fire-and-forget feature. They are an operated feature. The dashboards, metrics, and traces you build for debugging are the difference between a feature you maintain and one that maintains you.

meta.title: Self-host
meta.subtitle: The outbox pattern bridges your domain transactions and the webhook queue. The worker pool drains it. Behind nginx with TLS, on a VPS, with all the operational pieces from chapter 9 wired in.

meta.title: WebSockets & realtime — Roadmap
meta.subtitle: Ten chapters that go from 'what is the WebSocket handshake' to a self-hosted, horizontally scaled realtime server with Redis pub/sub, presence, auth, and nginx in front.

meta.title: What WebSockets are and when to use them
meta.subtitle: A WebSocket is a bidirectional, persistent TCP connection that starts life as an HTTP request. Useful when neither REST nor RPC fits. Misused often.

meta.title: The handshake and frame protocol
meta.subtitle: RFC 6455 in the parts that matter for shipping a server. Upgrade headers, frame layout, masking, opcodes, close codes, and the rules a library follows so you do not have to.

meta.title: Your first server
meta.subtitle: End-to-end Go WebSocket server in eighty lines, with a browser client. By the end you have an echo service plus a tiny chat room — both running on localhost, both real.

meta.title: Message protocols on top
meta.subtitle: Raw frames carry bytes. Real apps need types, versions, and request/response correlation. Designing the message envelope before you have ten clients in the wild saves you years of pain.

meta.title: Server-Sent Events
meta.subtitle: When the client only listens, SSE beats WebSockets in every dimension that matters — simpler protocol, free reconnect, plain HTTP. The default for one-way realtime.

meta.title: Pub/sub at scale
meta.subtitle: One process can hold thousands of connections; production needs many. The piece that makes that work is a pub/sub bus that fans events from any process to every connection on every other process.

meta.title: Presence and rooms
meta.subtitle: Knowing who is online and which channel they are watching looks easy in a one-process demo and is genuinely hard at scale. The data model is half the work; the eviction story is the other half.

meta.title: Auth, origin, and rate limits
meta.subtitle: WebSocket handshakes look like normal HTTP, which makes them subject to normal HTTP attacks plus a few WebSocket-specific ones. Verify origin, authenticate at the handshake, and rate-limit at every level you can.

meta.title: Backpressure, reconnects, heartbeats
meta.subtitle: Networks drop. Clients stall. Tabs sleep. The patterns in this chapter are the difference between a WebSocket service that runs for a week and one that limps for an hour.

meta.title: Production self-host
meta.subtitle: Behind nginx with TLS, systemd-managed, observable, scaled across processes via Redis. Same operational shape as the GraphQL and gRPC tracks — different protocol on the wire.
